coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of novel carbon dots from taurine for Cu2+ sensing and nanohybrid with ceria for visible light photocatalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Anirudh
Tejwan, Neeraj
Thakur, Saloni
Sharma, Vinit
Singh, Th. Abhishek
Das, Joydeep
coredata.dc:description: Here, we have prepared a new water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) from a conditionally essential amino acid, “taurine” via thermal decomposition method. The as-synthesized CDs displayed oval/spherical shape with size less than 10 nm. The existence of nitrogen, sulphur and oxygen in the CD was confirmed by x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The CDs exhibited a quantum yield value of 7.2% and excitation dependent fluorescence (FL) emission spectra, and they were used for sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ ions with a limit of detection of 0.62 μM. Furthermore, the aqueous solution of the CDs showed significant stability up to 90 days with very negligible effect on their fluorescence intensity under high salt concentration. After that we have prepared the nanohybrid (CDs-CeO2) consisting of CDs (0.025–0.3 wt %) and CeO2 as a visible light photocatalyst for the removal of methyl orange (MO) dye from water. The nanohybrid consisting of 0.1 wt % CDs displayed highest (66%) degradation towards MO at pH ∼ 6 under visible light irradiation for 5 h. However, 85% and 100% MO degradation were observed in strongly basic (pH ∼ 11) and acidic (pH ∼ 3) medium respectively. The enhanced photodegradation ability of the 0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2 is attributed to the highest visible light absorbing capacity and lowest band gap energy. Superoxide radicals (O2 −.) were found to play the primary role in the photocatalysis process within the pH range of 3–6. Besides, good stability and reusability of the nanocatalysts were also confirmed. Taken together our synthesized CDs and its nanohybrid with CeO2 can be considered as a promising candidate for sensing and photocatalytic activities.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of novel carbon dots from taurine for Cu2+ sensing and nanohybrid with ceria for visible light photocatalysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we have prepared a new water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) from a conditionally essential amino acid, “taurine” via thermal decomposition method. The as-synthesized CDs displayed oval/spherical shape with size less than 10 nm. The existence of nitrogen, sulphur and oxygen in the CD was confirmed by x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The CDs exhibited a quantum yield value of 7.2% and excitation dependent fluorescence (FL) emission spectra, and they were used for sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ ions with a limit of detection of 0.62 μM. Furthermore, the aqueous solution of the CDs showed significant stability up to 90 days with very negligible effect on their fluorescence intensity under high salt concentration. After that we have prepared the nanohybrid (CDs-CeO2) consisting of CDs (0.025–0.3 wt %) and CeO2 as a visible light photocatalyst for the removal of methyl orange (MO) dye from water. The nanohybrid consisting of 0.1 wt % CDs displayed highest (66%) degradation towards MO at pH ∼ 6 under visible light irradiation for 5 h. However, 85% and 100% MO degradation were observed in strongly basic (pH ∼ 11) and acidic (pH ∼ 3) medium respectively. The enhanced photodegradation ability of the 0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2 is attributed to the highest visible light absorbing capacity and lowest band gap energy. Superoxide radicals (O2 −.) were found to play the primary role in the photocatalysis process within the pH range of 3–6. Besides, good stability and reusability of the nanocatalysts were also confirmed. Taken together our synthesized CDs and its nanohybrid with CeO2 can be considered as a promising candidate for sensing and photocatalytic activities.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new kind of nanomaterial in nanocarbon family and have attracted much attention in recent research particularly in the field of sensing, bio-imaging, photocatalysis as well as energy because of their unique optical, electronic and catalytic properties [1–3]. CDs are defined as carbon nanomaterials with sizes ranging below 10 nm, and consist of either crystalline or amorphous core with sp2 carbon. Several heteroatom containing functional groups are also present on the carbon surface. CDs are considered as a better replacement for semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes owing to their low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, hydrophilicity, and stable photoluminescence [4,5]. Besides, CDs also show excitation-dependent emission; therefore, based on the requirements, the fluorescence emission wavelength can be tuned from UV to near-infrared region by altering the excitation wavelength. Further, several metal ions such as Cu2+, Cr6+, Au2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+ and Ag+ [1,2,6] can selectively quench the fluorescence intensity of CDs depending on the selective interaction between those ions and the surface functional groups on CDs. Therefore, CDs can be used for the sensitive and selective detection of metal ions by carefully choosing the precursor or by selective surface passivation. Detection and expulsion of the heavy metals by using CDs could be a potential approach for the waste water treatment [3,4,7]. However, the photoluminescent quantum yield (QY) of CDs is highly dependent on its precursor, nature of doping agent, surface defects as well as size [8,9].

The doping of heteroatoms can further increase the electronic and catalytic activity of CDs. Heteroatom doped (N and/or S) CDs exhibit small band gap as well as intensed optical absorption in the visible region, therefore can be used as photocatalyst. However, the catalytic performance of semiconductor catalysts can also be increased following conjugation with CDs due to their photosensitizing nature. For example, Yu and Kwak (2012) showed visible light-active photocatalytic activity of CDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposites fabricated by hydrothermal process [10]. Hang et al. (2012) reported the application of CDs/ZnO nanocomposites fabricated by hydrothermal process as an efficient photocatalyst under visible light exposure [11]. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites, has also been reported by Yu et al. against Methylene Blue (MB) degradation [12]. Recently, Pei et al. (2020) fabricated highly efficient visible light-active graphene quantum dots/CeO2 nanocomposites for photodegradation of rhodamine B [13]. Nanoceria or CeO2 NPs is a very popular photocatalyst and its light absorption threshold can reach 420 nm, which is higher than TiO2. However, its wide band gap (3.2 eV) limits its catalytic performance in practical purposes. Doping with noble metals (Au, Ag) [14,15] or alkali metals (Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba) [16–19] can improve their photocatalytic efficiency for the degradation of organic pollutants. So far, there is no report on the visible light-active photocatalytic efficiency of CDs/CeO2 nanocomposites for the degradation of methyl orange dye which is contained in the wastewater effluents from textile, leather and paper printing industries.

Primarily two main synthetic approaches are used for the synthesis of CDs; (a) top-down (carving large carbon structures into nanosize structures), and (b) bottom-up (building up of nanosize structures from suitable organic precursors) [5]. The bottom-up methods show several advantages in tuning the composition and physicochemical properties of CDs via careful selection of the carbon source as well as carbonization conditions. A verity of renewable and non-renewable precursors such as; gum arabic, fruit & vegetable juice, grass [6], glucose, ethylene diamine, citric acid, and amino acids [5,20,21], etc. have widely been used for the fabrication of CDs. However, further study is required to search for a novel, easily available and cheap precursor which can serve as both a carbon as well as a heteroatom source, and to develop an easy, cost effective, rapid and large-scale production of highly photoluminescent CDs. Currently amino acids are considered as the most promising carbon source due to their non-toxic nature and the presence of heteroatoms within their structure, therefore no additional surface passivation or doping is required. 2-aminoethane sulfonic acid or taurine is a conditionally essential amino acid and present in meat, dairy products, fish and milk, and is also available in energy drinks [22,23]. It can also protect human beings from heart disease, high blood pressure, high cholesterol, maintain calcium haemostasis and improve vision. Besides, taurine is lost cost and easily available commercially, therefore highly preferable for the large-scale production of CDs.

Herein, we reported a facile thermal decomposition method to synthesize novel water soluble CDs from taurine and used them for sensing Cu2+ ions in water. Furthermore, we have prepared nanohybrids composed of CDs and CeO2 NPs via the same method with different weight percentages of CDs, and compared their photocatalytic efficiencies for the degradation of methyl orange dye under visible light irradiation at room temperature.

Taurine (99%), sodium chloride, calcium chloride, cupric chloride, manganese (II) chloride, cobalt (II) chloride, nickel (II) chloride, ferrous chloride, ammonium chloride, barium chloride, benzoquinone, benzoic acid, NaHCO3, and K2Cr2O7 were purchased from “Loba Chemie” Pvt. Ltd. (Maharashtra, India), NaOH (98%) from “RANKEM” RFCL Ltd. (Thane, Maharashtra). Ammonium ceric nitrate (98%), cuprous chloride and potassium chloride were purchased from Central Drug House Pvt. Ltd. (New Delhi, India). TiO2 P25 was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All the chemicals were received and used without any further purification. REMI/R–8C machine was used for centrifugation. Disinfected deionized water with conductivity 18 Ω cm−1 is used in this study.

CDs were prepared using thermal decomposition method as described by Cruz et al., 2017 with minor modifications [24]. In brief, 4 g taurine was transferred in a 50 mL crucible and heated at 350 °C for 15 min in a muffle furnace manufactured by Laby instruments industry, Ambala Cantt, India. Following cooling down, the black coloured compound (CDs) was collected, suspended in deionized water, and washed 4 times to eliminate insoluble impurities via centrifugation process for 10 min at 4900 rpm. Finally, the resulting solution was lyophilized using a lyophilizer (Allied Frost, New Delhi, India) to get black CDs dry powder (3.621 g).

CeO2 was synthesized via thermal decomposition method [24]. In brief, 1.2 g NaOH aqueous solution (31.5 mL) was added to the aqueous 4.38 g ammonium ceric nitrate solution (5 mL) in a drop-wise manner under continues stirring condition to get yellow precipitates of cerium hydroxide. Then the water-soluble ionic impurities were removed via washing with deionized water. Thereafter, the as-prepared precipitates were collected in a 50 mL crucible and heated in a muffle furnace at 350 °C for 14 min. After cooling down, yellow CeO2 powder was obtained (1.32 g).

The nanohybrid was synthesized using the same procedure as reported above [24]. The as-obtained cerium hydroxide precipitates were suspended in water and mixed with taurine (0.125, 0.25, 0.5, 1 and 1.5 g) under continues stirring. The different reaction mixtures were then taken in 50 mL crucibles and heated in a muffle furnace at 350 °C for 14 min to get 0.025 wt % CDs-CeO2, 0.05 wt % CDs-CeO2, 0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2, 0.2 wt % CDs-CeO2 and 0.3 wt % CDs-CeO2 respectively. After cooling down to room temperature, brown coloured compounds were obtained. The nanohybrids were further suspended in deionized water, washed 4 times to eliminate free CDs, and dried in the oven at 50 °C.

Morphology and size of the synthesized nanoparticles were checked using a transmission electron microscope (FEI; FP-5022 Technai G20s-Twin, USA). Elemental examination was performed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (PHI 5000 Versa Prob II, FEI Inc. analyser). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was recorded on a FTIR spectrometer (Agilent technologies, L1600312: CA, USA) using pressed KBr pellets. The UV–visible and fluorescence spectra were recorded by using UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Wensar: LMSP-UV1900; Chennai, India) and spectroflurimeter (Varioskan LUX 3020–563, Waltham, USA) respectively. The quantum yield (Q.Y.) of CDs was calculated using quinine sulphated as a standard [25].

At first, we have checked the concentration-dependent fluorescence emission of CDs within the concentration range of 0.1–2 g/mL. The effect of ageing of CDs solutions (1 mg/mL, pH-7) on its fluorescence intensity was measured in the interval of 15 days. The effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of the aqueous solution of CDs (1 mg/mL) was investigated at the pH values 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, and 13. The pH values of the ultrapure water were adjusted using NaOH (10 mM) or HCl (10 mM) solutions. The effect of NaCl conc. (0.1–1 Μ) on the fluorescence intensity of the aqueous solution of CDs (1 mg/mL) was investigated at pH 7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of Cu2+ ions using CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing of aqueous Cu2+ ions was performed at room temperature fluorescence quenching method. Various concentrations of Cu2+ ions ranging from 0 to 25 μM were added to the aqueous solution of CDs (0.5 mg/mL), mixed well and kept for 10 min under dark. The fluorescence emission was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The fluorescence quenching of CDs in presence of other metal ions (Co2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Cu+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Mn2+, NH4+, Ca2+, and Ba2+) was also checked at a concentration of 25 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by using the formula as shown below:
LOD = 3 * standard deviation/slope.

Interference experiments were also performed to prove the selectivity of CDs toward Cu2+ sensing. For the interference experiments, various metal ions (Ca2+, NH4+, Na+, Fe2+, Cu+, Ni2+, K+, Ba2+, Mg2+, and Mn2+ with final concentration of 25 μM) were added to the CDs solution in presence of Cu2+ (25 μM), mixed well and the change in fluorescence emission was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm following incubation of 10 min at room temperature.

The photocatalytic performance of CDs, CeO2, TiO2 P25 and the nanohybrids (CDs-CeO2) was tested by studying the degradation kinetics of methyl orange (MO) under visible light irradiation. The photodegradation reaction was carried out in a 5 mL borosilicate glass tube. Typically, 3 mg of each photocatalyst was dispersed in 3 mL aqueous solution of MO (1 mg/100 mL). Deionized water (pH ∼ 6) was used for the photodegradation experiments. Prior to visible light irradiation, the reaction mixtures were stirred for about 1 h under dark condition to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium in between MO and photocatalysts. Subsequently, the stable aqueous reaction mixtures were irradiated to visible light at room temperature for different time intervals (1–5 h) using a visible fiber lamp (Panasonic 23 WT-LED bulb) under continues stirring. Finally, aliquots were taken, centrifuged to remove heavy particles, and the concentration of MO dye was analyzed by monitoring the change in the absorbance at 464 nm. Similar reaction mixtures of all the composites, CDs, CeO2, and TiO2 P25 were prepared without MO dye for blank substruction. Bare MO dye solution with same concentration was used as a standard. The degradation rate of MO was calculated using the formula: % degradation = (Ablank – Asample/Ablank) * 100.
Ablank is the initial absorbance of MO, and Asample is the absorbance of MO after certain time interval of photocatalytic reaction.

The catalyst (0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2) with highest photocatalytic activity is further tested for the recycling performance up to 5 h. After each cycle, the catalyst was collected by centrifugation, washed, dried and used for further catalysis reactions. The band gap energies of CeO2 and their composites were determined by plotting the graph between (αhν)2 versus photon energy (hν). Here, α (alpha) is the absorbance calculated from UV spectrophotometer and hν is calculated from wavelength using the formula: (hv = 1240/wavelength). The band gap energies were obtained from the intercept of the curves on the horizontal axis. Furthermore, we have investigated the photocatalytic mechanisms of action of 0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2 towards MO degradation by using various scavengers/sacrificial agents at a final concentration of 1 mg/mL and pH 6 or 3. In this study, Benzoquinone [26,27], K2Cr2O7 [28], benzoic acid [29], and NaHCO3 [30] were employed as scavengers of O2 −., e−, OH., and h+, respectively.

The electrochemical measurements were performed with FRA-2 μAUTOLAB TYPE III system by using a standard screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE), reference Ag/AgCl electrode and carbon counter electrode. The solution for electrode modification was prepared by suspending 2 mg of the materials in 2 mL acetone containing Nafion (a binder) followed by sonication for 30 min. The electrochemical analyses were carried out by using 60 μL of the KMnO4 solution dropped onto the surface of the modified SPCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and catalyst concentration on MO degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on the MO degradation was examined in the presence of 0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2 photocatalyst and TiO2 P25 after 5 h of visible-light irradiation. The pH values of the reaction mixtures were adjusted using NaOH (10 mM) or HCl (10 mM) solutions. The effect of photocatalyst (0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2) concentrations was examined in the range of 0.5–2.0 mg/mL after 5 h of visible-light irradiation.
We performed each experiment at least 3 times and the results are expressed as mean ± SD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CDs, CeO2 as well as different CDs-CeO2 hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, we have described the fabrication of a novel CDs from taurine, CeO2 from ammonium ceric nitrate, as well as nanohybrids of CDs and CeO2 with different compositions, and checked their applications in the field of metal ion sensing and photocatalysis (Fig. 1 ). The morphology of CDs, CeO2 as well as 0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2 was analyzed by TEM (Fig. 2 a–c) which demonstrated nearly spherical shape of the nanoparticles. Size of the as-synthesized CDs was less than 10 nm (Fig. 1S), whereas size of CeO2 was ranging from 20 to 50 nm. The XPS analysis confirmed the coexistence of cerium (Ce3d, 882.93 eV), oxygen (O1s, 529.95 eV), nitrogen (N1s, 400.09 eV), carbon (C1s, 285.26 eV), and sulphur (S, 169.09 eV) in the nanohybrid (0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2) (Fig. 2d). The presence of characteristic functional groups in taurine, CDs, CeO2 and 0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2 was monitored by FTIR spectral analysis (Fig. 2e). Taurine and the CDs exhibited various characteristic transmittance peaks at around 510 cm−1, 594 cm−1, 734 cm−1, 1034 cm−1, 1174 cm−1, 1205 cm−1, 1508 cm−1, 1614 cm−1, 2968 cm−1, 3036 cm−1, and 3196 cm-1 CeO2 exhibited a characteristic transmittance peak at 484 cm−1 and various other transmittance peaks at 1626 cm−1, 1456 cm−1, 1303 cm−1, and a broad band centred at 3355 cm−1 were found. Fig. 3 a displayed the XRD pattern of CDs, CeO2 and 0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2. CDs exhibited a broad peak centred at 2θ value of 23°. On the other hand, both CeO2 and CDs-CeO2 exhibited several high intensity peaks at 2θ values 28.76, 32.89, 47.52, 56.48, 59.1, 69.57, 77.65 and 79.1 which are assigned to (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), (400), (331) and (420) crystal planes. Raman spectra of CDs, CeO2 and CDs-CeO2 were shown in Fig. 3b. CDs showed the two peaks near 1391 cm−1, and 1546 cm−1, whereas CeO2 displayed characteristic peak near 458 cm−1. Besides, CDs-CeO2 exhibited both the characteristic peaks of CDs and CeO2.

Fig. 3c showed the UV-VIS spectra of CeO2 as well as the nanohybrids with different CD and CeO2 compositions. Bare CDs showed absorption peaks at 275, 340, 375 and 450 nm, while bare CeO2 NPs showed a broad band centred at 325 nm (Fig. S2a), whereas no characteristic peak was observed for taurine itself. However, the absorption intensity of all the nanoparticles except 0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2 was very less in the visible region, whereas 0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2 showed around five times increased absorption intensity as compared with bare CeO2. Besides, 0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2 showed a red shift in absorption edge towards the visible region (at around 450 nm), whereas other nanoparticles showed their absorption edge at around 300 which is far behind the visible region. The CDs exhibited an excitation dependent (320–440 nm) fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 4 a) and the maximum fluorescence intensity around 450–500 nm following excitation at 360 nm. The quantum yield (Q.Y.) of CDs was calculated to be 7.2% using quinine sulphated as a standard. In addition, the fluorescence nature of the CDs was also checked by using a fluorescence microscope which showed blue (UV), green (FITC), and red (RITC) fluorescence when excited at ultraviolet, blue and green light region (Fig. S2b).

At first the fluorescence intensity of CDs was checked with different concentrations (0.1–1 mg/mL), and it was found to be increased in a concentration-dependent manner from 0.1 to 1 mg/mL. However, stable emission intensity was observed thereafter with no significant change with concentration up to 2.0 mg/mL (Fig. 4b). After that the effect of ageing of the CDs solution (aqueous) was checked on their fluorescence intensity, and no significant decrease was observed after storage up to 3 months at room temperature (Fig. 4c). The effects of pH and ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity were also investigated (Fig. 4 d-e). Fig. 4d depicted a slight increase in fluorescence emission intensity with increase in pH values from 3 to 13. On the other hand, as illustrated in Fig. 4e the fluorescence intensity was not significantly changed at different ionic strengths in NaCl solution from 0.1 to 1 M.

Here, we have also checked the potential of CDs to be used for sensing of metal ions in aqueous media via fluorescence quenching. At first, we have compared the change in fluorescence intensity of CDs by adding various metal ions at a concentration of 25 μM by recording the emission spectra at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Fig. 5 a depicted the change in fluorescence intensities of CDs in terms of F (fluorescence intensity in presence of metal ions)/F0 (fluorescence intensity in absence of metal ions) ratio. We observed that Cu2+ caused maximum quenching in the fluorescence intensity among all the tested metal ions. Further, we have also checked the detection limit of CDs within the Cu2+ concentration range of 0.3–25 μM by plotting the relative change in fluorescence intensity, (F0 – F)/F0 with concentrations. We observed a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs with increasing concentration of Cu2+ ions (Fig. 5b). (F0 – F)/F0 showed a good linear correlation with Cu2+ concentrations in the range of 0.3–25 μM (Fig. 5c) with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.98. The limit of detection was calculated to be 0.62 μM by using the linear regression equation (F0 – F)/F0 = 0.101 x CCu + 0.021 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Interference experiments were also performed to prove the selectivity of CDs toward Cu2+ sensing in presence of other interfering ions (Fig. 5d). However, very little interference was observed from the different metal ions, and the quenching effect of Cu2+ was obvious.

To demonstrate the potential of the as-synthesized CDs-CeO2 nanohybrids as effective photocatalyst under visible light irradiation, we have checked the photodegradation of methyl orange (MO) dye as an organic pollutant. The photodegradation abilities of various CDs-CeO2 nanohybrids were compared with bare CeO2, and TiO2 P25 (a standard photocatalyst) at pH∼6 in a time-dependent manner as depicted in Fig. 6 a. CeO2 and their composites with different amount of CDs time-dependently increased the degradation of MO, whereas the highest degradation was observed for 0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2 after 5 h of light irradiation. CeO2 and TiO2 P25 could only degrade up to 35% and 23% of MO respectively, whereas 0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2 degraded up to 66% of the dye. The catalytic efficiency was found in the order: 0.1 wt % > 0.2 wt % > 0.3 wt % ∼0.05 wt % > 0.025 wt % ∼ CeO2 > TiO2 P25. The UV–visible spectra (Fig. 6b) and photograph (Fig. 6c) of MO solution clearly showed the time-dependent (1–5 h) degradation of the dye using 0.1 wt % CDs–CeO2 under visible light irradiation. The MO dye removal under dark condition was also depicted in Fig. S3a, which showed that the adsorption-desorption equilibrium between MO and the nanohybrids was established at 1 h, and no significant change was observed up to 5 h. We have also compared the fluorescence intensity of CDs and their hybrids with CDs (0.1, 0.2 and 0.3 wt % CDs-CeO2 nanohybrids) (Fig. S3b). Pure CDs and CDs-CeO2 nanohybrids exhibited similar characteristic fluorescence spectra, but the fluorescence intensity of pure CDs was higher than their composites with CeO2. 0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2 exhibited the maximum fluorescence quenching as compared with pure CDs (2-fold). The extent of fluorescence quenching was found in the order: 0.1 wt % > 0.2 wt % > 0.3 wt %.

To further explore the mechanism of the photocatalytic efficiency of CDs–CeO2 (0.1 wt %), capture experiments were performed. As shown in Fig. 6d, at pH 6, when benzoquinone (O2 −. scavenger) was added into the reaction mixture, the photodegradation of MO was significantly decreased, whereas very little inhibition was observed following the addition of K2Cr2O7 (e− scavenger) and NaHCO3 (h+ scavenger). On the other hand, no effect was observed after the addition of benzoic acid (OH. scavenger). All these experimental results suggested that O2 −. is the primary active species in the photocatalytic process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical band gap and electrochemical measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The optical band gap energies of bare CeO2 and their hybrids were calculated by using Tauc plot method (Fig. 7 a). Bare CeO2 showed a band gap energy value of 3.18 eV, where as this value was decreased after conjugation with CDs. 0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2 exhibited the minimum value of 2.40 eV, whereas 0.2 wt % and 0.3 wt % ∼ CDs-CeO2 exhibited the values at 2.82 eV and 2.93 eV respectively. To examine the charge transfer resistance of the prepared materials, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was performed. 0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2 exhibited the minimum arc radius of the Nyquist curve among all the prepared materials (Fig. 7b). The cyclic voltammograms (CV) showed that the redox current density of 0.3 wt % CDs-CeO2 was the largest among all the prepared samples (Fig. 7c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and concentration on MO degradation
ce:section.ce:para: We have further checked the effect of catalyst (0.1 wt % CDs–CeO2) concentration on MO degradation (Fig. 8 a). We observed that the degradation efficiency of the catalyst is increased with increasing concentration from 0.5 to 1.5 mg/mL, and decreased thereafter at higher concentration (2 mg/mL). The highest MO degradation (approx. 72%) was obtained at a catalyst concentration of 1.5 mg/mL under visible light exposure for 5 h. Fig. 8b depicted the reusability of the photocatalyst (0.1 wt % CDs–CeO2) under the similar conditions. The catalyst showed MO degradation rate of around 62% and 57% after 2nd and 3rd consecutive photodegradation reactions. We have further checked the effect of solution pH on MO photodegradation. Fig. 8c showed the % degradation of MO using 0.1 wt % CDs–CeO2 in the order pH-3 > pH-11> pH-6 after 5 h of visible light exposure with 100%, 85% and 66% degradation efficiency respectively. It was also found that the photocatalytic efficiency of the catalyst (0.1 wt % CDs–CeO2) was so increased that it could trigger complete MO degradation within 25 min at pH-3 (Fig. 8d–e). However, TiO2 P25 could only degrade nearly 20% of MO under the same condition. We have further carried out the capture experiments to identify the active species in the photodegradation of MO at pH 3 (Fig. 8f). We have observed the most significant inhibition in the photocatalytic activity after the addition of benzoquinone, whereas K2Cr2O7 and NaHCO3 showed moderate inhibition upon MO degradation. On the other hand, benzoic acid exhibited very little effect on MO degradation. Therefore, O2 −. is again playing the primary role in the photocatalytic process at pH 3.

The last two decades have witnessed an upsurge of research in the field of CDs and their composites due to their enormous chemical & biological applications. In the present work, we have utilized thermal decomposition method to prepare a novel water-soluble CDs using an amino acid, taurine and explored their application as a sensor for Cu2+ detection. Thereafter, nanohybrids of CDs with CeO2 NPs were synthesized with different weight % of CDs, and investigated their photocatalytic activity for the degradation of methyl orange (MO) dye as an organic pollutant.

All the prepared samples were characterized by TEM, XPS, FTIR, and UV spectroscopy. TEM analysis revealed nearly spherical shape for CDs (<10 nm) and CeO2 (20–50 nm). The CDs-CeO2 nanohybrids (0.1 wt % CDs–CeO2) exhibited the coexistence of both CDs and CeO2 NPs. The XRD spectrum of the as-synthesized CDs and CeO2 NPs indicated the amorphous nature of CDs and cubic fluorite structure of CeO2 NPs [6,14]. However, in the CDs-CeO2 nanocomposite, the peak intensities of CeO2 were decreased and few peaks were overlapped. Raman spectrum of CDs-CeO2 showed three peaks at 461 cm−1, 1402 cm−1, and 1557 cm−1, which correspond to F2g vibration mode of oxygen atom in cerium oxide as well as D-band and G-band of the carbon material respectively [31]. The D-band corresponds to the vibration of the carbon atom with dangling bonds, whereas G-band is related to the E2g mode of graphite [32].

We have further confirmed the existence of CDs and CeO2 in the nanohybrid (0.1 wt % CDs–CeO2) by XPS analysis which revealed the coexistence of Ce, N, O, and S. FTIR analysis showed the characteristic transmittance peaks/bands of taurine, CDs, CeO2, and 0.1 wt % CDs-CeO2 nanohybrids. The transmittance peaks of taurine at 3196 and 3036 cm−1 were assigned to symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching of N–H bond; 2968 cm−1 was assigned to C–H stretching; 1614 and 1508 cm−1 were assigned to symmetrical and asymmetrical NH2 deformation; 1205, 1174 and 1034 cm−1 were assigned to symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching of SO3 and C–N stretching respectively, 734 cm−1 was assigned to C–S stretching; and 594 and 510 cm−1 were assigned to symmetrical and asymmetrical SO3 deformation [33,34]. It was observed that all these characteristic peaks of taurine were present in CDs. In case of CeO2 NPs, a transmittance peak cantered at 484 cm−1 was assigned to Ce–O stretching vibrations. CeO2 NPs also exhibited the broad bands located ∼3400 cm−1, 1456 cm−1, and 1303 cm−1 were assigned to the adsorbed water and CO2 molecules [35]. However, CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid showed reduced intensity of Ce–O stretching which might be attributed to the interaction of Ce3+/4+ and the NH2/SO3H groups.

From the UV–visible spectra, we have observed three absorption peaks at ∼275 nm as well as 340 nm and 375 nm which were appeared due to π → π* transition of the aromatic CC/CN sp2 domains and n → π* transition of CO/CN bonds [36,37]. Besides, a weak absorption peak was also appeared at 450 nm due to π → π* transition of CS bonds. The Bare CeO2 exhibited an absorption band centred at 325 nm [35]. The absorption intensity of CeO2 in the visible region was very less and it was increased following composite formation with CDs. However, the most dramatic increase in absorption intensity was observed with 0.1 wt % CD-CeO2 hybrids with significant red shift in their absorption edge towards visible region (450 nm) as compared with bare CeO2 (300 nm). This broadening of absorption edge to the visible region is attributed to the π- π conjugation of CDs, and may significantly enhance the photocatalytic activity under visible light illumination.

The fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs demonstrated an excitation dependent emission pattern when excited within the wavelength range of 320–440 nm. The fluorescence emission of CDs not only varied in intensity but also shifted towards the longer wavelength (red shift) with increasing excitation wavelengths. The CDs showed maximum emission intensity near 450–500 nm following excitation at 360 nm. The quantum yield of CDs was 7.2% using quinine sulphated as a standard. Besides, the aqueous solution of CDs showed a stable fluorescence with no significant decay upon storage for 90 days at room temperature. The fluorescence stability of CDs was further checked in presence of different pH and ionic strength conditions. The CDs displayed a slight increase in fluorescence intensity upon increasing the pH from 3 to 13. This kind of pH-dependent increase in fluorescence intensity is already reported by Mohammed and Omer in 2020 [38]. In addition, the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs was almost unchanged at different NaCl concentrations up to 1 M, thereby indicating good stability under high salt concentrations.

Here, we have utilized the fluorescence property of CDs for sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ ions. Several researchers showed that CDs with amine functional groups are very effective nano-sensor for the selective detection of Cu2+ ions due to their high affinity towards amine groups [39,40]. In our present study, Cu2+ ions have shown a very powerful FL quenching ability than other metal ions at similar concentration, thereby proving that the nano-sensor is more selective for Cu2+ ions. Besides, the relative change in FL intensity, (F0 – F)/F0 with Cu2+concentrations was also checked to determine the sensitivity of the nano-sensor and limit of detection (LOD) value. (F0 – F)/F0 showed a good linear correlation with Cu2+ concentrations in the range of 0.3–25 μM and the LOD value was calculated to be 0.62 μM. The performances of other CD-based sensor for Cu2+ detection is summarized in Table 1 , and the obtained LOD value with our synthesized CDs is comparable or even higher than many other reported CDs. The coexistance of other metal ions did not show any significant interference, thereby indicating high selectivity and good anti-interference efficacy of the CDs for the detection of Cu2+. The most accepted mechanisms of fluorescence quenching are believed to occur via the inner filter effect (IFE) and formation of cupric amine complexes [41,42]. IFE is defined as the re-absorbtion of the emitted energy from CDs by Cu2+ ions, thereby decreasing the intensity of fluorescence emission from CDs. Besides, the photoexcited CDs can form complex with Cu2+ ions by donating the electrons of nitrogen to the Cu2+ ions, consequently the intensity of fluorescence emission from CDs may decrease.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy further showed improved electron-hole separation efficiency for the 0.1 wt % CDs–CeO2 nanohybrid which displayed the minimum arc radius in the Nyquist curve, indicating smallest transmission resistance of electrons and enhanced electrical conductivity [44–47]. Besides, the cyclic voltammogram of the 0.3 wt % CDs–CeO2 coated electrode showed highest current density [48].

The sample displaying the highest photocatalytic performance (0.1 wt % CDs–CeO2) was selected for further optimization of reaction conditions. The MO degradation was increased with the increase in photocatalyst concentration up to 1.5 mg/mL and decreased thereafter might be due to the limited interaction of light with photocatalyst at higher concentrations [4,5,13,14,49–51]. Furthermore, we have also observed that the photocatalytic performance of the nanohybrid was increased under both acidic and basic conditions as the change in pH alters the surface property CeO2 catalyst. The catalyst showed 100% and 85% MO degradation at pH∼3 and pH∼11 respectively. The increased photocatalytic activity of the nanohybrid in basic condition might be caused due to the increased generation of hydroxyl (HO . ) radicals in addition to superoxide radicals (O2 −.) [52,53]. On the other hand, the acidic medium favors’ increased adsorption of the dye on positively charged catalyst surface, thereby causing increased dye degradation [54,55]. Besides, MO exist in the quinonoid form at acidic pH and favors its degradation in absence of stable azo bond [56]. The catalyst also showed MO removal rate of around 57% after three consecutive photodegradation reactions at pH∼6 and is decreased by only 12% after the first photocatalysis reaction, thereby indicating good stability and reusability of the catalyst.

In conclusion, we prepared a novel water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) with QY of 7.2% from a conditionally essential amino acid “taurine” via thermal decomposition method. The CDs showed excitation dependent fluorescence (FL) emission and good stability over a long period of time and in the presence of high NaCl concentration. The CDs were used for sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ ions with a detection limit of 0.62 μM. The coexistance of other metal ions did not show any significant interference, thereby indicating high selectivity and good anti-interference efficacy of the CDs for the detection of Cu2+. Further, we have prepared the nanohybrids consisting of CDs (0.025–0.3 wt %) and CeO2 and used them for the photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange (MO) dye under visible light irradiation. The nanohybrid consisting of 0.1 wt % CDs displayed highest (66%) MO degradation at pH ∼ 6 among all the tested photocatalysts. Besides, the nanocatalyst exhibited 100% MO degradation in acidic medium within 25 min as well as good stability and reusability. Superoxide radicals (O2 −.) were found to play the primary role in the photocatalysis process within the pH range of 3–6. Overall, the present work focuses on the synthesis of a novel water-soluble CDs and its nanohybrid with CeO2 for sensing and photocatalytic activities.

Anirudh Sharma: Methodology, performed the optical measurements, characterized the nanoparticles, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Neeraj Tejwan: performed the optical measurements, characterized the nanoparticles. Saloni Thakur: performed the optical measurements. Vinit Sharma: performed the electrochemical analysis, All authors reviewed the manuscript. Th Abhishek Singh: characterized the nanoparticles. Joydeep Das: Methodology, Supervision, the study, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft.
